risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The randomization list and numbered packing of the intervention, allocating patient in 1:1 ratio to either SCT or non SCT groups, were prepared off site by central data coordinator, for all centres. The random numbers were generated by a computer programme using permuted blocks of variable length." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated random numbers with permuted blocks of variable length), which supports truly random allocation.

Quote: "An external data management centre was responsible for random allocation of patients, data entry and data management." and "The randomization list and numbered packing of the intervention, allocating patient in 1:1 ratio to either SCT or non SCT groups, were prepared off site by central data coordinator, for all centres." Comment: The article explicitly describes off-site preparation by a central data coordinator/external data management centre, indicating a central allocation process separated from recruiters at study sites. This is an explicit concealment mechanism that would prevent the person enrolling participants from foreseeing the upcoming assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The present clinical trial was a Phase III prospective, open labelled, randomized, multicentric trial..." and "The patients randomized to SCT group received intracoronary stem cell infusion after successful completion of PCI..." Comment: The study explicitly states it was open labelled, so participants and treating personnel were not blinded to allocation. Because the dominant outcomes include LVEF improvement and reported adverse events/hospitalization/chest pain/dyspnoea, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence co-interventions, care, symptom reporting, and performance-related aspects of these outcomes. Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "The present clinical trial was a Phase III prospective, open labelled, randomized, multicentric trial..." and "An independent external observer, not involved in the study, reviewed MUGA scans at all centers... The nuclear medicine specialists in all centers and the independent external observer were blinded to each other and patient assignment." Comment: The key assessed outcome was left ventricular ejection fraction measured by MUGA, with assessment by nuclear medicine specialists and an independent external observer; this is an imaging-based outcome and assessors were explicitly blinded to patient assignment. Secondary outcomes were death, repeat AMI, cerebral infarction, revascularization, hospitalization and serious adverse events, which are largely objective/record-based rather than subjective self-report scales. No subjective patient-reported or clinician-rated scales were reported. Therefore, lack of participant blinding in this open-label trial is unlikely to have biased outcome measurement under this domain.

Quote: "Two hundred and fifty patients were randomly assigned... to a non SCT group (n: 125)... or to a SCT group (n: 125)... In the SCT group, 114 patients received the stem cells... The final cohort followed up for six months included, 109 patients in SCT group and 117 in non-SCT group... In the non-SCT group eight were lost to follow up and in the SCT group there were 53 trial deviates (Fig. 1)." Quote: "Analysis of the primary outcome was done using the intention-to-treat analysis..." Comment: Post-randomization outcome data at 6 months were not complete: 109/125 analyzed in SCT and 117/125 in non-SCT for the primary LVEF outcome, implying 16/125 (12.8%) vs 8/125 (6.4%) not in the final 6-month cohort. However, the paper does not clearly account for all missing SCT participants or explain how the stated ITT analysis handled these missing outcome data. The reported "trial deviates" are partly protocol deviations/suboptimal dosing rather than straightforward missing outcome data, and the per-arm reasons for all non-analyzed participants are not fully transparent. Attrition is not high enough, based on reported numbers, to clearly establish high risk, but reporting is insufficient to confirm low risk.
